docs.title: Flex Documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the official documentation for Flex, a "smaller brother" of sUNC.

This documentation site serves as the central knowledge base for understanding what Flex is, how it works, how it differs from sUNC, and how to contribute to the project.

Whether you're an executor developer, a curious tester, or a contributor helping document functions, you're in the right place!

A standard, separate from sUNC

Flex is a distinct standard from sUNC, providing a space for Quality of Life (QoL) functions that don't fit sUNC's strict function inclusion criteria. This helps avoid bloating sUNC with convenience functions while offering proper testing and documentation for those that are still valuable.

docs.title: 📚 Explore the Docs
docs.text:
• What is Flex?
  • How Flex test results work
  • How to contribute

Thank you for being here.

docs.title: Contribution Guide
docs.text:
Welcome to the Flex documentation project! We worked tirelessly to standardise and make the documentation beautiful and readable for everyone.

This guide outlines the official standards for contributing to the Flex documentation, including how libraries and functions should be structured and written.

Consistency makes the documentation readable, searchable, and enjoyable to explore.

docs.title: Documenting entire libraries
docs.text: Each library must be placed in a folder named after the library itself. Inside this folder, you must include a README.md file, which serves as the index page for that library, in both this documentation website and also when browsing the documentation repository on GitHub.

docs.title: Index page requirements
docs.text:
• Introduce the purpose of the library
  • Summarise what you can and cannot do with it

Index pages do not need to follow this strict format, but they should aim to do so, for clarity and helpfulness.

docs.title: General documentation style
docs.text:
All documentation pages (including function pages and index pages) must:

  • Use inline links to relevant Wikipedia, Roblox Luau, or other Flex pages with referencing key terms.

  • Use the correct inline link formats, e.g.:

    [`#!luau task.defer`](https://create.roblox.com/docs/reference/engine/libraries/task#defer)

    As you can see, the example above uses a code snippet with luau syntax highlighting enabled, whilst also being a hyperlink to the Roblox API documentation for task.defer.

All markdown documents must be formatted using David Anson's "markdownlint" extension, available for free in the Visual Studio marketplace.

This extension alone is not sufficient enough, which is why we use Yu Zhang's "markdown all in one" extension too, but only specifically for formatting tables. This makes tables look nice and readable in the source markdown files of the documentation.

The repository for this documentation already includes a .vscode/extensions.json file, so the extensions should automatically download for you once you consent.

When formatting tables, open the Command Palette with Ctrl + Shift + P (Windows) or ⌘ + Shift + P (macOS), select "Format Document With...", and select "Markdown All In One" like so:

docs.title: Using British English
docs.text: It is generally well known around the sUNC & Flex community that the documentation and most things that have a lot of text are written using British English. This usually means some words Americans spell with a "z" will instead use an "s". If you are unsure, please check the Oxford or Cambridge dictionaries.

docs.title: The "Oxford Comma"
docs.text:
Please use the Oxford Comma when writing lists. This means that in a list of three or more items, a comma is placed before the final "and" or "or". For example: "apples, oranges, and bananas" rather than "apples, oranges and bananas".

The Oxford Comma avoids ambiguity and makes lists clearer. It also mirrors natural pauses in daily speech, which makes the text easier to read aloud and less confusing to follow.

docs.title: Short-form contractions
docs.text: Please avoid contractions like "isn't" and "it's". Instead, use "is not" and "it is" respectively. These are just examples, but the same rules apply for all contractions. Because this documentation is formal and technical rather than conversational, contractions should be avoided.

docs.title: Articles before a word
docs.text:
An article is something that goes before a word, like a and an. This is a polite reminder to not just put an an in front of things that have a vowel literally, but rather whether the word has a vowel sound when pronounced out loud. Non-native English speakers tend to get this rule wrong sometimes.

If unsure about pronunciation, assume that pronunciation is done with a British RP accent - the best sources to listen to are the Oxford and Cambridge dictionaries again where the pronunciation is perfect.

docs.title: Function documentation
docs.text: Each function must have its own file, named after the function (e.g. newcclosure.md).

docs.title: Title Format
docs.text:
The title of the page must be written as:

# `function_name`

Important

Do not include emojis in function titles.

docs.title: Notices
docs.text: Place all important !!! callouts (warnings, errors, info) immediately under the title. These should include anything the user must know before using the function.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Provide a description of the function directly after any notices.

When referencing the function name, always use the code snippet format:

`#!luau function_name` allows you to do this and that.

docs.title: Type definition
docs.text:
Type definitions are important!

You must always include a Luau-style type definition when documenting a function.

Include the Luau type definition at the bottom of the first section, with Luau syntax highlighting but no individual line highlighting or line numbers:

function newcclosure<A..., R...>(functionToWrap: (A...) -> R...): (A...) -> R...

Make sure it precedes the parameters table.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
The parameters must always be neatly described in a function, like so:

## Parameters

| Parameter               | Description                         |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| `#!luau parameter_name` | Short description of the parameter. |

Use snake_case for every variable, and camelCase for parameters to easily distinguish between the two.

There should be no horizontal rule (---) between the type definition and the parameters section.

docs.title: Providing users with examples
docs.text:
Every function page must include at least one example.

Use 'Example' or 'Examples' based on how many examples you have

To make the documentation experience more logical and also grammatically correct, please do the following: - Make the heading ## Example if there is only one example. - Make the heading ## Examples if there is more than one example. Each example should have its own subheading, e.g. ### Example 1, ### Example 2.

Each example should use the following format:

## Example

```luau title="Short but descriptive title for your code" linenums="1"
-- an example is here
print("Hello world!")
print("This is some example code")
```
  • Examples must use luau syntax highlighting.
  • Line numbers must be enabled using linenums="1".
  • Each example must have a title describing in short what the code does.

docs.title: How do test results work?
docs.text: Flex is known for its strict tests. Just like sUNC, ever since it was made, it was built to expose falsified environments by actually testing for functionality - not merely checking for their presence.

docs.title: Using the dedicated game & helper
docs.text:
All tests must be conducted in the official Flex testing game, and must be accompanied by the Flex Helper - an external application used to verify functionality that cannot be reliably tested from the Luau environment.

The official game may be found in our Discord server.

Distribution of the Flex Helper

The Flex Helper is ONLY distributed via flex.gd/helper, and NOWHERE ELSE.

If you encounter any impersonation or unofficial distribution, report it immediately to legal@numelon.com.

A dedicated testing environment allows Flex to provide online, verifiable test results via Rubiš, ensuring results are reproducible and trustworthy, instead of people sending meaningless screenshots of their console output.

The Flex Helper exists to extend testability beyond the limitations identified in sUNC.

Certain functionality is inherently ambiguous or "untestable" within Luau alone. For example, the Drawing library can be partially vaidated by sUNC to see if the correct object structure is returned, but it cannot determine whether rendering has actually occurred on-screen.

The Flex Helper resolves this limitation by enabling external verification of such behaviour.

docs.title: What is Flex?
docs.text: Flex is a separate standard from sUNC, designed as its "little brother" to provide a space for Quality of Life (QoL) functions that do not meet sUNC's strict inclusion criteria.

docs.title: Purpose and Design
docs.text:
While sUNC rigorously excludes functions that can be implemented in Lua or are deemed unnecessary, Flex offers a compromise by allowing select convenient functions that enhance user experience without compromising on testing quality. This approach addresses numerous community requests for additional functions while maintaining the integrity of sUNC as a lean, essential standard.

Flex remains strict in its own right, carefully curating only a small set of valuable QoL functions rather than adding numerous bloated features. These functions still undergo proper testing.

docs.title: Relation to sUNC
docs.text: Flex exists independently from sUNC to prevent the core standard from being overwhelmed and/or bloated by convenience-focused additions. Functions in Flex are thoroughly documented and tested, providing users with reliable QoL enhancements that sUNC intentionally omits. This separation allows sUNC to remain focused on essential functions while Flex caters to somewhat practical needs.
